elements.sent-2411-001: We show that any continuous semi-group on $L^1$ which is (i) $L^1-$contractive, (ii) satisfies the conservation law $\partial_t \rho+\partial_x(H(x,\rho))=0$ in $\R_+\times (\R\backslash\{0\})$ (for a space discontinuous flux $H(x,p)= H^l(p) {\bf 1 _{x<0 + H^r(p) {\bf 1 _{x>0}$), and (iii) satisfies natural continuity and scaling properties, is necessarily given by a germ condition at the junction: $\rho(t,0)\in \mathcal G$ a.e., where $\mathcal G$ is a maximal, $L^1-$dissipative and complete germ.}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: In a symmetric way, we prove that any continuous semi-group on $L^\infty$ which is (i) $L^\infty-$contractive, (ii) satisfies with the Hamilton-Jacobi equation $\partial_t u+H(x,\partial_x u)=0$ in $\R_+\times (\R\backslash\{0\})$ (for a space discontinuous Hamiltonian $H$ as above), and (iii) satisfies natural continuity and scaling properties, is necessarily given by a flux limited solution of the Hamilton-Jacobi equation.
elements.sent-2411-003: The aim of this note is to identify the conservation law (CL) semi-groups and the Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) semi-groups which are $L^1-$ (for the CL) or $L^\infty-$ (for HJ) contracting, in the context of a 1:1 junction.
elements.sent-2411-004: Namely we consider a discontinuous flux (or Hamiltonian) $H:\R\times \R\to \R$ of the form \begin{equation}H(x,p)= H^l(p){\bf 1}_{\{x<0\}}+ H^r(p){\bf 1}_{\{x>0\}}\end{equation} and look at the CL \begin{equation}\partial_t \rho+ \partial_x (H(x,\rho))= 0 \qquad in\; \R_+\times (\R\backslash \{0\})\end{equation} or the HJ equation \begin{equation}\partial_t u+ H(x,\partial_x u)= 0 \qquad in\; \R_+\times (\R\backslash \{0\}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-005: The problem is to find conditions that have to be put at the junction $x=0$ to ensure that the equation is well-posed and $L^1-$contractive (for the CL) or $L^\infty-$contractive (for the HJ).
elements.sent-2411-006: Many papers have built such semi-groups for the CL: see, for instance, \cite{AGDV11, AKR11, AuPe05, BaJe97, BKT09, GNPT07, To00, To01 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: The main contribution of \cite{AKR11 is to identify a general class of such semi-groups, expressed through the notion of $\mathcal G-$entropy solutions: the additional condition takes the form $(\rho(t,0^-), \rho(t,0^+))\in \mathcal G$ for a.e.}
elements.sent-2411-008: A somewhat symmetric construction is done in \cite{IM17 for the HJ equation, introducing the notion of flux limited solutions: the additional condition is now takes the form: \begin{equation}\partial_t u + \min \{\bar A, (H^l)^+(\partial_x^- u), (H^r)^-(\partial_x^+u)\}= 0 \qquad in \; (0, \infty)\times \{0\},\end{equation} for a suitable flux limiter $\bar A$ (see below for the notations).}
elements.sent-2411-009: The aim of this short note is to show that these semi-groups---described through germs (for CL) or flux limiters (for HJ)---are actually the only contractive ones.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our main results (Theorem \ref{thm.main1 for the HJ equation and Theorem \ref{thm.main2 for the conservation law) state that, if a continuous semi-group is $L^1-$ or $L^\infty-$ contractive, coincides locally with the CL or HJ semi-group outside $x=0$ and satisfies natural continuity and scaling properties, then it is necessarily given by a germ (for the CL) or a flux limiter (for the HJ).}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The choice to present at the same time the case of CL and of HJ is related to the fact that there is a strong relationship between both semi-groups: see \cite{CFGM (for convex Hamiltonians) and \cite{FIM24 (for coercive ones).}}
elements.sent-2411-012: We make a use of this property in the proofs.
elements.sent-2411-013: These results are somewhat expected: indeed, the introduction of \cite{AKR11 suggests that the notion of $\mathcal G-$entropy solutions was introduced to describe all the $L^1-$dissipative semi-groups for conservation law on an 1:1 junction.}
elements.sent-2411-014: On the other hand, \cite{FIM24, IM17 show that, for the HJ equation, general conditions of the form $\partial_tu +F(\partial_x u)=0$ at the junction can be expressed in terms of flux limiter conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The main interest of this note is to actually {\it prove the fact that natural contraction properties are enough to identify the semi-groups.}
elements.sent-2411-016: To fix the ideas we work in the framework of traffic flow, i.e., assume that the fluxes/Hamiltonians $H^{l/r}$ are strictly concave.
elements.sent-2411-017: The results can probably be extended to more general fluxes or Hamiltonians, but to the expense of more technical proofs.
elements.sent-2411-018: Let us finally say a word about more complex junctions, i.e., junctions with more than one incoming branch or more than one outgoing one.
elements.sent-2411-019: The situation is then much more delicate: approaches by HJ and by CL are no longer equivalent (see for instance a counterexample in \cite{CFGM ), and the class of conditions that have to be put at the junction is much richer and---yet---far from understood.}
elements.sent-2411-020: We fix two smooth, Lipschitz and uniformly concave Hamiltonians $H^{l : [0,R^l]\to \R$ and $H^r: [0,R^r]\to \R$, with $H^l(R^l)=H^r(R^{r )=H^l(0)=H^r(0)=0$ (for some $R^l, R^r>0$) and set \begin{equation}H(x,p)= H^l(p){\bf 1}_{\{x<0\}}+ H^r(p){\bf 1}_{\{x>0\}}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: We define $Lip$ as the set of Lipschitz maps $u:\R\to \R$ with $u'\in [0,R^l]$ a.e.
elements.sent-2411-022: The set $Lip$ is endowed with the topology of local uniform convergence.
elements.sent-2411-023: We denote by $\bar S^{HJ}$ the semi-group associated with the Hamilton-Jacobi equation on the line: given $u_0\in Lip$, $u(t,x):= \bar S^{HJ (t,u_0)(x)$ is the {\it minimal solution to \begin{equation}\left\{{l} \partial_t u +H(x,\partial_xu) = 0 \qquad in\; (0,\infty)\times \R u(0,x)= u_0(x) \qquad in\; \R \right.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Then $u(t,\cdot)\in Lip$ for any $t\geq0$ and $\bar S^{HJ}$ is a continuous semi-group.
elements.sent-2411-025: Note that, if $H^l=H^r$, then $\bar S^{HJ}$ the standard semi-group associated with the Hamilton-Jacobi equation on the line.
elements.sent-2411-026: Here we need to specify that it is the minimal solution as the Hamiltonian is discontinuous.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our aim is to identify any semi-group $S^{HJ}$ satisfying the following conditions: \begin{equation}(H1) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}:[0,\infty)\times Lip\to Lip$ is a continuous homogeneous semi-group,} (H2) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}$ is $L^\infty-$contractive: if $u_0^1, u_0^2\in Lip\cap L^\infty(\R), \; \|S^{HJ}(t,u^0_1)-S^{HJ}(t,u_0^2)\|_\infty \leq \|u_0^1-u_0^2\|_\infty$,} (H3) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}$ permutes with constants: $S^{HJ}(t, u_0+c)= S^{HJ}(t,u_0)+c$ for any $c\in \R$ and $u_0\in Lip$,} (H4) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}$ has a finite propagation property: there exists $C_0>0$ such that,} \qquad \text{ if $u_0^1= u_0^2$ on $[a,b]$, then $S^{HJ}(t,u^0_1)= S^{HJ}(t,u_0^2)$ on $[a+C_0t, b-C_0t]$ for $t\in[0, C_0^{-1}(b-a)]$, } (H5) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}$ coincides locally in $\R_+\times \R\backslash \{0\}$ with $\bar S^{HJ}$: for any $u_0\in Lip$ and $x\neq 0$, } \qquad \qquad \text{ $S^{HJ}(t,u_0)(y)=\bar S^{HJ}(t,u_0)(y)$ for any $t\in (0, C_0^{-1}|x|)$ and $y\in \R$ with $|y-x|<|x|-C_0t$,} (H6) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}$ is scale invariant: for any $\ep,t >0$ and $u_0\in Lip$, $\ep S^{HJ}(t/\ep, \ep u_0(\cdot/\ep))(x/\ep)= S^{HJ}(t,u_0)(x)$.}\end{equation} \begin{Theorem Under the conditions above, there exists $\bar A\in [0,A_{\max ]$ such that $S^{HJ =S^{HJ,\bar A}$, where $A_{\max =\min\{\max H^l, \max H^r\}$ and $S^{HJ,\bar A}$ is the HJ semi-group with flux-limiter $\bar A$, i.e., for a given initial condition $u_0\in Lip$, the viscosity solution to \be \left\{\begin{array {l \partial_t u +H(x,\partial_xu) = 0 \qquad \text{in}\; (0,\infty)\times (\R\backslash\{0\}) \partial_t u + \min \{\bar A, (H^l)^+(\partial_x^- u), (H^r)^-(\partial_x^+u)\}= 0 \qquad \text{in \; (0, \infty)\times \{0\} u(0,x)= u_0(x) \qquad \text{in}\; \R \end{array}\right.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}(H1) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}:[0,\infty)\times Lip\to Lip$ is a continuous homogeneous semi-group,} (H2) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}$ is $L^\infty-$contractive: if $u_0^1, u_0^2\in Lip\cap L^\infty(\R), \; \|S^{HJ}(t,u^0_1)-S^{HJ}(t,u_0^2)\|_\infty \leq \|u_0^1-u_0^2\|_\infty$,} (H3) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}$ permutes with constants: $S^{HJ}(t, u_0+c)= S^{HJ}(t,u_0)+c$ for any $c\in \R$ and $u_0\in Lip$,} (H4) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}$ has a finite propagation property: there exists $C_0>0$ such that,} \qquad \text{ if $u_0^1= u_0^2$ on $[a,b]$, then $S^{HJ}(t,u^0_1)= S^{HJ}(t,u_0^2)$ on $[a+C_0t, b-C_0t]$ for $t\in[0, C_0^{-1}(b-a)]$, } (H5) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}$ coincides locally in $\R_+\times \R\backslash \{0\}$ with $\bar S^{HJ}$: for any $u_0\in Lip$ and $x\neq 0$, } \qquad \qquad \text{ $S^{HJ}(t,u_0)(y)=\bar S^{HJ}(t,u_0)(y)$ for any $t\in (0, C_0^{-1}|x|)$ and $y\in \R$ with $|y-x|<|x|-C_0t$,} (H6) \qquad \text{$S^{HJ}$ is scale invariant: for any $\ep,t >0$ and $u_0\in Lip$, $\ep S^{HJ}(t/\ep, \ep u_0(\cdot/\ep))(x/\ep)= S^{HJ}(t,u_0)(x)$.}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-028: The notion of flux-limited viscosity solution of \eqref{Eq.HJfluxlimitedCC is defined as usual, except that the test functions $\phi=\phi(t,x)$, which are continuous in $\R_+\times \R$ and $C^1$ in $\R_+\times (\R\backslash \{0\})$, have only a left- and right- space derivative at $x=0$, denoted respectively by $\partial_x^- \phi(t,0)$ and $ \partial_x^+ \phi(t,0)$.}
elements.sent-2411-029: This notion of viscosity flux limited solution for \eqref{Eq.HJfluxlimitedCC was introduced in \cite{IM17 , which also established that, for any $A\in [0, A_{\max ]$, there exists a unique viscosity solution to \eqref{Eq.HJfluxlimitedCC .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Recall finally that, by \cite{CT80 , under (H3), (H2) is equivalent to a comparison principle: $u_0^1\leq u_0^2\; \Rightarrow \; S^{HJ (t,u^0_1)\leq S^{HJ (t,u_0^2)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: To simplify notation, we set throughout the proof $S=S^{HJ}$, $\bar S=\bar S^{HJ}$ and $S^A=S^{HJ,A}$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Let us start with preliminary remarks.
elements.sent-2411-034: For $u_0\in Lip$, the map $(t,x)\to S(t,u_0)(x)$ is continuous and a solution of the HJ equation in $(0,\infty)\times (\R\backslash \{0\})$: by \cite[Theorem 2.7]{IM17 it is therefore a supersolution to the HJ equation on the whole space $(0,\infty)\times \R$.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We infer by comparison that $S(t,u_0)\geq \bar S(t,u_0)$.
elements.sent-2411-036: Given $A\in [0, A_{\max ]$, let $p^{r/l,- _A$ and $p^{r/l,+ _A$ be respectively the smallest and the largest solutions to $H^{r/l (p)=A$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: We define the maps \begin{equation}\check \phi_A(x)= p^{l,-}_A x {\bf 1}_{x\leq 0}+ p^{r,+}_A x {\bf 1}_{x\geq 0}, \qquad \hat \phi_A(x)= p^{l,+}_A x {\bf 1}_{x\leq 0}+ p^{r,-}_A x {\bf 1}_{x\geq 0} .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\check \phi_A(x)= p^{l,-}_A x {\bf 1}_{x\leq 0}+ p^{r,+}_A x {\bf 1}_{x\geq 0}, \qquad \hat \phi_A(x)= p^{l,+}_A x {\bf 1}_{x\leq 0}+ p^{r,-}_A x {\bf 1}_{x\geq 0} .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-039: Indeed, the map $w(t,x):= \hat \phi_A(x)- tA$ is a classical solution to the HJ equation in $(0,\infty)\times (\R\backslash\{0\})$ and satisfies the initial condition.
elements.sent-2411-040: As for the condition at the vertex, it holds in a classical sense since \begin{equation}\partial_t w(t,0) + \min \{ A, H^{l,+}(\partial_x^- w(t,0)), H^{r,-}(\partial_x^+w(t,0))\} = -A + \min \{ A, H^{l,+}(p^{l,+}_A), H^{r,-}(p^{r,-}_A)\} = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\partial_t w(t,0) + \min \{ A, H^{l,+}(\partial_x^- w(t,0)), H^{r,-}(\partial_x^+w(t,0))\} = -A + \min \{ A, H^{l,+}(p^{l,+}_A), H^{r,-}(p^{r,-}_A)\} = 0.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-041: The idea of the proof is to first identify $S(t,\cdot)$ for the particular functions $\check \phi_A$ and $\hat \phi_A$, and then to use the $\check \phi_A$ and $\hat \phi_A$ as test functions.
elements.sent-2411-042: We do this through several steps: we first compute $S^A(t,\hat \phi_0)$ (Step 1), then define $\bar A$ and show, thanks to the first step, that $S(t, \hat \phi_{0 )= S^{\bar A (t,\hat \phi_0)$ (Step 2): as $u\geq \hat \phi_0+ u(0)$ for any $u\in Lip$, this will give the supersolution property.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: To prove the subsolution property, we show that $S(t, \check \phi_A) = S^{\bar A (t, \check \phi_A)$ (Step~3).}
elements.sent-2411-044: We conclude in Step~4 by the proof of the equality $S=S^{\bar A}$.. {\it Step 1.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We first claim that, for any $A\in [0,A_{\max ]$, \be S^A(t,\hat \phi_0)(x)= \left\{\begin{array {ll \hat \phi_A(x)-tA \text{if}\; x\in [t(H^l)'(p^{l,+ _A), t(H^r)'(p^{r,- _A)], \bar S(t, \hat \phi_0)(x) \text{otherwise \end{array}\right.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: We claim that \begin{equation}\bar S(t,\hat \phi_0)(x) = \left\{{ll} 0 if \; x\geq t (H^r)'(0), - t (H^l_{A_{\max}})^*(x/t){\bf 1}_{x\leq 0} -t (H^r_{A_{\max}})^*(x/t){\bf 1}_{x\geq 0} if \; x\in [ t (H^l)'(R^l), t (H^r)'(0)], R^lx if \; x\leq t (H^l)'(R^l), \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\bar S(t,\hat \phi_0)(x) = \left\{{ll} 0 if \; x\geq t (H^r)'(0), - t (H^l_{A_{\max}})^*(x/t){\bf 1}_{x\leq 0} -t (H^r_{A_{\max}})^*(x/t){\bf 1}_{x\geq 0} if \; x\in [ t (H^l)'(R^l), t (H^r)'(0)], R^lx if \; x\leq t (H^l)'(R^l), \right.}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-047: Indeed, let us denote by $w(t,x)$ the function in the right-hand side.
elements.sent-2411-048: Then $w$ is continuous at $(t,0)$ because \begin{equation}w(t,0^\pm)= -t(H^{l/p}_{A_{\max}})^*(0) = -t\sup_{y\in [0,R]} \min\{H^{l/p}(y), A_{\max}\}= -tA_{\max}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-049: Moreover, $w$ is continuous at $(t, x)$ for $x:=t (H^r)'(0)>0$ since \begin{equation}w(t,x^-)=- t(H^r_{A_{\max}})^*(x/t)= - t\sup_{y\in [-R,0]} -(H^r)'(0)y+\min\{H^r(y), A_{\max}\}= tH^r(0)=0,\end{equation} because the maximum in the second formula is reached at $y=0$ by the optimality condition.
elements.sent-2411-050: In the same way, for $x=t(H^l)'(R)<0$, \begin{equation}w(t,x^+)=-t(H^l_{A_{\max}})^*((H^l)'(R))= t(H^l)'(R^l)R^l= R^lx.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-051: In addition, $w$ is actually $C^1$ outside $x=0$, because $w$ is $C^1$ outside $x\in \{t (H^l)'(R^l), t (H^r)'(0),0\}$ and \begin{equation}\partial_x w(t, (t(H^r)'(0))^-)= -((H^r_{A_{\max}})^*)'((H^r)'(0))= 0,\end{equation} while $\partial_x w(t, (t(H^l)'(R^l))^+)= -((H^l_{A_{\max )^*)'((H^l)'(R^l))=R^l$.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: In addition, for $x=t (H^r)'(0)$, \begin{equation}\partial_t w(t,x^-)= -(H^r_{A_{\max}})^*(x/t)+ (x/t) ((H^r_{A_{\max}})^*)'(x/t)= - (H^r_{A_{\max}})^*((H^r)'(0))+ (H^r)'(0) ((H^r_{A_{\max}})^*)'((H^r)'(0))= 0,\end{equation} and, for $x= t(H^l)'(R^l)$, \begin{equation}\partial_t w(t,x^+)= -(H^l_{A_{\max}})^*((H^l)'(R^l))+ (H^l)'(R^l) ((H^l_{A_{\max}})^*)'((H^l)'(R^l))= (H^l)'(R^l)R^l - (H^l)'(R^l) R^l= 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-053: One also easily checks that $w$ satisfies the HJ equation in $(0,\infty)\times (\R\backslash\{0\})$ in a classical sense and the initial condition.
elements.sent-2411-054: Finally, at $x=0$, \begin{equation}\partial_t w(t,0)+ \min \{ A_{\max}, H^{l,+}(\partial_x^- w(t,0)), H^{r,-}(\partial_x^+w(t,0))\} = -A_{\max} + A_{\max}=0,\end{equation} since $\partial_x^- w(t,0)= -((H^l_{A_{\max )^*)'(0^-)=p^{l,- _{A_{\max }$ and $\partial_x^+w(t,0) = p^{r,+ _{A_{\max }$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\partial_t w(t,0)+ \min \{ A_{\max}, H^{l,+}(\partial_x^- w(t,0)), H^{r,-}(\partial_x^+w(t,0))\} = -A_{\max} + A_{\max}=0,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-056: Next we denote by $u$ the function in the right-hand side of \eqref{ilkjzerdsCC .}

elements.sent-2411-060: Indeed, for $x= t(H^l)'(p^{l,+ _A)\in [ t (H^l)'(R^l), t (H^r)'(0)]$, \begin{equation}\hat \phi_A(x)-tA = p^{l,+}_A t(H^l)'(p^{l,+}_A)- t \min\{H^l(^{l,+}_A), A_{\max}\}= -t (H^l_{A_{\max}})^*((H^l)'(^{l,+}_A))= w(t,x).\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-085: We next claim that, for any $A\in [0,\bar A]$, \begin{equation}S(t, \check \phi_A) (0)=-At.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-119: As in the previous part, we fix two smooth, Lipschitz and uniformly concave Hamiltonians $H^{l : [0,R^l]\to \R$ and $H^r: [0,R^r]\to \R$, with $H^l(R^l)=H^r(R^{r )=H^l(0)=H^r(0)=0$ (for some $R^l, R^r>0$) and set \begin{equation}H(x,p)= H^l(p){\bf 1}_{\{x<0\}}+ H^r(p){\bf 1}_{\{x>0\}}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: We now denote by $L^\infty$ the set of measurable maps $\rho$ such that $\rho(x)\in [0,R^l]$ a.e.
elements.sent-2411-121: We endow the set $L^\infty$ with the topology of the $L^1_{loc}$ convergence.
elements.sent-2411-123: Our aim is to identify any semi-group $S^{CL}$ on $L^\infty$ satisfying the following properties: \begin{equation}(H1) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}:[0,\infty)\times L^\infty\to L^\infty$ is a continuous homogeneous semi-group,} (H2) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}$ satisfies the $L^1$ contraction property: for any $u^1_0,u^2_0\in L^\infty\cap L^1(\R)$, } \qquad \qquad \text{$\|S^{CL}(t,u^0_1)- S^{CL}(t,u_0^2)\|_{L^1(\R)}\leq \|u^1_0-u^2_0\|_{L^1(\R)}$,} (H3) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}$ preserves mass: if $\rho_0\in L^\infty\cap L^1(\R)$, then $\int_\R S^{CL}(t, u_0)= \int_\R u_0$ for any $t\geq 0$,} (H4) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}$ has a finite speed of propagation: there exists $C_0>0$ such that,} \qquad \qquad\text{ if $u_0^1= u_0^2$ on $[a,b]$, then $S^{CL}(t,u^0_1)= S^{CL}(t,u_0^2)$ on $[a+C_0t, b-C_0t]$ for $t\in[0, C_0^{-1}(b-a)]$, } (H5) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}$ coincides locally with $\bar S^{CL,l/r}$ away from $x=0$: for any $u_0\in L^\infty$ and $x\neq 0$, } \qquad \qquad \text{$S^{CL}(t,u_0)(y)=\left\{ {ll} \bar S^{CL,l}(t,u_0)(y) if\; x<0 \bar S^{CL,r}(t,u_0)(y) if\; x>0 \right.$ for any $(t,y)$ with $|y-x|<|x|-C_0t$,} (H6) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}$ is scale invariant: for any $\ep,t >0$ and $u_0\in L^\infty$, $ S^{CL}(t/\ep, u_0(\cdot/\ep))(x/\ep)= S^{CL}(t,u_0)(x)$.}\end{equation} \begin{Theorem Under the conditions above, there exists $\bar A\in [0,A_{\max ]$ such that $S^{CL =S^{CL,\bar A}$, where $A_{\max =\min\{\max H^l,\max H^r\}$ and $S^{CL,\bar A}$ is the conservation law semi-group with flux-limiter $\bar A$, i.e., for a given initial condition $\rho_0\in L^\infty$, the entropy solution to \be \left\{\begin{array {l \partial_t \rho +\partial_x(H(x,\rho)) = 0 \qquad \text{in}\; (0,\infty)\times (\R\backslash\{0\}) (\rho(t,0^-),\rho(t,0^+))\in \mathcal G_{\bar A \qquad \text{a.e.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-032: \begin{equation}(H1) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}:[0,\infty)\times L^\infty\to L^\infty$ is a continuous homogeneous semi-group,} (H2) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}$ satisfies the $L^1$ contraction property: for any $u^1_0,u^2_0\in L^\infty\cap L^1(\R)$, } \qquad \qquad \text{$\|S^{CL}(t,u^0_1)- S^{CL}(t,u_0^2)\|_{L^1(\R)}\leq \|u^1_0-u^2_0\|_{L^1(\R)}$,} (H3) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}$ preserves mass: if $\rho_0\in L^\infty\cap L^1(\R)$, then $\int_\R S^{CL}(t, u_0)= \int_\R u_0$ for any $t\geq 0$,} (H4) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}$ has a finite speed of propagation: there exists $C_0>0$ such that,} \qquad \qquad\text{ if $u_0^1= u_0^2$ on $[a,b]$, then $S^{CL}(t,u^0_1)= S^{CL}(t,u_0^2)$ on $[a+C_0t, b-C_0t]$ for $t\in[0, C_0^{-1}(b-a)]$, } (H5) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}$ coincides locally with $\bar S^{CL,l/r}$ away from $x=0$: for any $u_0\in L^\infty$ and $x\neq 0$, } \qquad \qquad \text{$S^{CL}(t,u_0)(y)=\left\{ {ll} \bar S^{CL,l}(t,u_0)(y) if\; x<0 \bar S^{CL,r}(t,u_0)(y) if\; x>0 \right.$ for any $(t,y)$ with $|y-x|<|x|-C_0t$,} (H6) \qquad \text{$S^{CL}$ is scale invariant: for any $\ep,t >0$ and $u_0\in L^\infty$, $ S^{CL}(t/\ep, u_0(\cdot/\ep))(x/\ep)= S^{CL}(t,u_0)(x)$.}}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-124: The notion of $\mathcal G-$entropy solution for \eqref{Eq.CLgerm is introduced in \cite{AKR11 : it is a Kruzhkov entropy solution in $(0,\infty)\times (\R\backslash\{0\})$, its trace at $t=0$ is $\rho_0$ and its trace at $x=0$ belongs to $\mathcal G_{\bar A}$ (the existence of a strong trace being given by \cite{Ps07, V01 ).}}}
